question: how to increase humic acid in soil
title: How to Increase Humic Acid in Soil: The Biological Way and the Shortcut
metaDescription: You can add humic acid products or you can grow your own. Here's the complete breakdown of both approaches — and why Scotty believes the biological method is the only one that actually lasts.
content:
# How to Increase Humic Acid in Soil: The Biological Way and the Shortcut

Humic acid is pretty much the gold standard of soil organic matter. It's the stable, dark, complex fraction of humus that's left after biology has thoroughly processed organic material — the end product of the decay cycle doing its full work. Soils with high humic content are, almost universally, the best-performing soils on earth.

So how do you get more of it?

Two approaches. The shortcut, which means purchasing commercial humic acid products and applying them. And the biological method, which means feeding the decay cycle and letting soil biology build humus the way it's been doing for millions of years. Both have their place. But they are not the same thing, and I want y'all to understand the difference.

## What Humic Acid Actually Is

Humic substances form through a process called humification — the slow, multi-stage decomposition of organic matter by bacteria, fungi, and other soil organisms. When a plant dies, the easy stuff — sugars, proteins, starches — gets broken down fast by bacteria. What's left is the harder stuff: cellulose, lignin, waxes. Fungi and actinomycetes go to work on those resistant compounds. Over months and years, through oxidation and polymerization and biological transformation, those resistant compounds get converted into complex organic molecules called humic substances: humic acid, fulvic acid, and humin.

Humic acid specifically is a large molecule that's soluble in alkaline conditions but precipitates in acidic conditions. Dark brown to black in color — which is why high-humus soils are so dark. It has an extraordinary capacity to bind to minerals (chelation), hold water, buffer pH, and support microbial life.

This stuff doesn't form quickly. It forms through time and biological activity and undisturbed soil. Albert Howard spent his career arguing that the goal of agriculture should be to sustain and build this humus fraction — and that modern farming, by removing organic matter without returning it, was destroying the accumulated humus of centuries.

## Approach 1: Commercial Humic Acid Products

The fastest way to increase humic acid in your soil is to add it directly through a commercial product. Most of these are derived from leonardite — a soft, oxidized lignite coal that's basically ancient humus, formed over geologic time from plant matter compressed in anaerobic conditions. High in humic and fulvic acids and dissolves readily when treated with an alkaline solution.

You can find it as granular humate, liquid humate concentrate, or dry water-soluble powder. All of these work. Applied at label rates, they genuinely improve soil CEC, water retention, and microbial activity.

The limitation is persistence. Commercial humic products get processed by soil biology over time — they don't have the molecular integration that naturally-formed humus in living soil has. They improve conditions but tend to require ongoing reapplication rather than building the way biological humus does.

For a starting point — especially in very degraded soils that need a biological kick-start — they're a legitimate tool. The research consistently shows measurable soil improvements from humate applications, even short-term.

## Approach 2: Compost — The Humus Factory

Compost is the most accessible biological pathway to increased humic acid in your soil. When you compost organic material, you're running the humification process in a compressed, managed form.

Hot composting — maintaining temperatures of 130 to 160°F through the pile — breaks down the fast-decomposing components rapidly. But the humic acid doesn't form in the hot phase. It forms in the curing phase — the cooler, slower period after the pile has passed through its heating cycles and is gradually maturing. Actinomycetes (those filamentous bacteria that give finished compost its earthy smell) and fungi are major producers of humic compounds. They need time and relatively stable, cooler conditions to do that work.

Aged compost is worth the wait. If you can let a compost pile cure for 6 to 12 months after it's finished heating, the humic content increases a good bit. You're not just making fertilizer — you're making humus precursors. You're making soil.

Here's the hard truth that EcoFarming Daily and others have documented: you cannot add enough compost to dramatically raise humic acid levels quickly. The amounts required are impractical for most gardeners. Compost is a slow, steady contributor to soil humus over years. That's why it works best as an ongoing practice rather than a one-time application.

## Approach 3: Cover Crops and Living Roots

This is the approach Gabe Brown leans hardest on in *Dirt to Soil*, and for good reason. Living roots are one of the most effective humus-building tools available.

Roots exude carbon compounds that feed soil biology — bacteria and fungi cluster around root zones in extraordinarily high concentrations. When roots die, they leave behind channels and organic matter at depth, below where tillage would reach. Deep-rooted species like daikon radish, sunflowers, and deep-tap legumes can deposit organic matter 12 to 24 inches down in the soil profile, building humus at depths where it's most protected from disturbance.

Cover crops also add massive amounts of biomass at the soil surface when they die or are cut. That surface biomass, left in place rather than removed, feeds the surface biology that's the first stage of humification. It mulches moisture, moderates temperature, and provides a continuous food source for the organisms that will, over time, transform it into humic compounds.

The key is continuity. Year-round soil coverage — never leaving soil bare — means the biology is always fed, always active, always building. Every season of cover cropping is a deposit into the humus account.

## Approach 4: Stop Tilling

Here's the one that costs nothing and might be the most important of all: stop breaking up your soil.

Humus formation requires undisturbed conditions for the slower biological processes to run their course. Every time you till, you're doing several damaging things at once. You're exposing organic matter to oxidation, which burns off carbon as CO2. You're destroying fungal networks — and fungi, especially the glomalin-producing mycorrhizal fungi, are major contributors to stable soil aggregation and humus accumulation. You're physically disrupting the microsites where humification is occurring.

Studies consistently show that no-till soils accumulate organic matter and humic substances faster than tilled soils under equivalent organic matter additions. No-till protects and builds. Tillage exposes and depletes.

For home gardeners, this means shifting toward a permanent bed system with defined pathways — you never walk on the beds, never disturb the structure except to plant and harvest. Add organic matter on top and let the biology move it into the profile. It's a little bit counterintuitive if you've been told that loose, tilled soil is what plants need. But what roots really need is biological channels and aggregate structure, not mechanically tilled looseness.

## Putting It Together

To genuinely increase humic acid in your soil over time:

Make compost and age it. Add it generously every season. Not as a quick fix — as an ongoing commitment to the biological system.

Use cover crops continuously. Something in the ground every season.

Stop tilling. Or minimize it as much as you can. Let biology do the structural work.

Add commercial humate products as a bridge amendment when you're starting from a depleted baseline, but know that these are supports for a biological process — not a substitute for it.

Albert Howard's Law of Return remains the best summary: what you take from the land, you must return. The decay cycle that returns organic matter builds humus. Build the decay cycle and the humus follows. That's not a theory. It's what the best soils on earth have been doing since before any of us were here.

## Sources

- Compost And Humic Substances — BioCycle
- Humic Acids vs. Compost — Huma Gro
- Compost and humic acid amendments are a practicable solution to rehabilitate weak arid soil — Scientific Reports / Nature
- A History of Organic Farming: Albert Howard's War in the Soil — Weston A. Price Foundation
